column4: The yield on short-term government bonds. Reflects monetary policy expectations and near-term economic outlook.
The yield on medium-term government bonds. The benchmark rate for much of the economy's borrowing.
The yield on long-term government bonds. Reflects long-run growth and inflation expectations.
The difference between long-term and short-term yields. An inverted curve often predicts recessions.
The weighted-average interest rate across all government debt. Blends short-term (15%), medium-term (55%), and long-term (30%) bond yields to reflect the true market cost of sovereign borrowing.
The proportion of government revenue consumed by interest payments on the national debt.
The perceived risk of government default. Higher risk means higher borrowing costs and capital flight.
The total amount of money circulating in the economy. Excess money supply drives inflation.
The total economic output measured in current prices, including the effects of inflation.
The government budget balance. High values indicate surplus, low values indicate deficit.
Economic output per person adjusted for purchasing power parity.
The extra yield investors demand for holding longer-term bonds instead of rolling short-term ones.
The cost of energy in the economy. Affects inflation, growth, and business confidence.
